stem: Why do fat protons resonate at a slightly lower frequency than water protons in the same main field?

options.text: Fat protons have a different gyromagnetic ratio from hydrogen in water
Electron shielding reduces the effective magnetic field experienced by fat protons
Water protons stop precessing during frequency encoding
Fat contains no hydrogen outside the selected slice

explanation: The molecular electron cloud shields fat protons slightly from B0, lowering their effective field and resonance frequency. The nuclei are still hydrogen and share the same gyromagnetic ratio.

objective: Relate chemical shift to field strength.
stem: How does the fat-water frequency separation in hertz change when field strength increases?

options.text: It decreases in inverse proportion to field strength
It remains constant in hertz at all fields
It increases in direct proportion to field strength
It disappears above 1.5 T

explanation: The chemical shift is approximately constant in parts per million, but the underlying Larmor frequency rises with B0. The absolute separation in hertz therefore increases at higher field.

stem: Where does spatial misregistration of fat relative to water appear?

options.text: Only along the slice-selection direction
Only along the phase-encoding direction regardless of readout
Equally in all directions without relation to gradients
Along the frequency-encoding direction

explanation: Type 1 chemical shift occurs because the reconstruction interprets fat's frequency offset as a position offset along readout. Swapping frequency and phase directions moves the artifact accordingly.

stem: Which change reduces the pixel displacement from type 1 chemical shift?

options.text: Increase receiver bandwidth
Decrease receiver bandwidth
Increase TE without changing bandwidth
Increase the phase matrix only

explanation: A wider bandwidth assigns more hertz to each pixel, so the fixed fat-water frequency difference spans fewer pixels. The tradeoff is reduced SNR.

stem: What produces a dark outline at fat-water interfaces on an opposed-phase image?

options.text: Spatial wrap of anatomy outside the phase FOV
Cancellation of fat and water signals within the same voxel
Failure of the 180-degree pulse to refocus static inhomogeneity
Loss of all longitudinal magnetization in pure fat

explanation: When fat and water magnetization are opposed in a mixed voxel, their transverse signals subtract and may cancel. This type 2 chemical shift effect is often called the India-ink artifact.
